options.claudeReactionPrompt: The rate lock expires. The new rate is 10416.00 IDR/AUD — 0.8% worse than the locked rate. The human would have received 62,496,000 IDR instead of 63,000,000 — a loss of 504,000 IDR. The agent re-locks at the inferior rate.

options.label: Switch to Alternative Provider
options.description: Cancel and use the alternative provider with a 0.5% worse rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Switching providers means restarting the entire process: new compliance checks, new KYC verification, and the alternative rate is already 0.5% worse. The time cost and inferior rate make this strictly worse than confirming at the locked rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent cancels and restarts with the alternative provider. New compliance verification takes 30 minutes. The rate is 0.5% worse, costing the human an additional A$30. The delivery timeline has slipped significantly past the end of week requirement.

steps.claudeContext: Rate locked at 10500 IDR/AUD, 45 seconds remaining. Transfer: A$6,000 → 63,000,000 IDR to Jeff in Jakarta. Confirm now, wait for better rate, or switch provider?
The transfer is processing via Wise. Tracking ID assigned. Jeff will be notified by SMS. Estimated delivery: 1-4 hours. The agent should decide whether to monitor the transfer or report completion.

decision.prompt: The transfer is in transit. How should the agent handle post-transfer monitoring?

options.label: Monitor Until Confirmed Delivered
options.description: Track the transfer until Jeff receives the funds, then report to the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Monitoring until delivery confirmation provides the human with end-to-end assurance. Cross-border transfers can encounter intermediary delays, compliance holds, or recipient bank issues. Active monitoring allows the agent to intervene quickly if problems arise.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent monitors the Wise transfer. After 2 hours, delivery is confirmed. Jeff in Jakarta has received 63,000,000 IDR for education sponsorship. The agent sends a confirmation to the human with the tracking details and exchange rate achieved.

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to send A$800 to Jeff in Lagos, Nigeria for construction project. Delivery requirement: within 4 hours. Begin by scanning available remittance corridors, compliance requirements, and FX rates for the Australia → Nigeria corridor.

Scanning the Australia → Nigeria corridor. Mid-market rate: 1 AUD = 1050 NGN. At A$800, the recipient would receive approximately 840,000 NGN. Compliance requirement: Building permit. Transfer is below AUSTRAC reporting threshold. Evaluate available rails and their trade-offs.

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 1
agentVisionData.value: Remitly: 2-3 hours, A$12 fee, rate markup 0.8%

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 2
agentVisionData.value: SWIFT gpi: 1-2 business days, A$25-35 fee, rate markup 0.2-0.4%

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 3
agentVisionData.value: Wise: 1-4 hours, A$5 fee, mid-market rate

decision.prompt: Which remittance rail should the agent use for the A$800 transfer to Nigeria?

options.label: Use Remitly (Recommended for this corridor)
options.description: Remitly is the optimal rail for the Nigeria corridor based on speed, cost, and reliability.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: For the Australia → Nigeria corridor with within 4 hours delivery and A$800 amount, Remitly is optimal. This rail offers competitive rates for Nigeria with reliable delivery. The corridor limit of A$8,000 accommodates this transfer.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent selects Remitly for the transfer. The quoted rate of 1050 NGN/AUD is locked for 60 seconds. Transfer fee: A$15. Jeff in Lagos will receive approximately 840,000 NGN. Proceeding to compliance verification.

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Traditional)
options.description: Route through traditional correspondent banking with SWIFT gpi tracking.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: SWIFT gpi is reliable but slower (1-2 business days) and more expensive (A$25-35 fee plus intermediary bank charges). For within 4 hours delivery to Nigeria, this may not meet the timeline. Correspondent bank fees can also reduce the received amount unpredictably.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent routes via SWIFT gpi. The transfer enters the correspondent banking chain: originator bank → intermediary → beneficiary bank in Nigeria. An intermediary bank deducts a $15 processing fee, reducing the amount received by Jeff. Delivery estimate: 1-2 business days.

options.label: Split Across Multiple Rails
options.description: Divide the transfer across multiple services to hedge against delays.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Splitting a transfer across rails adds complexity without clear benefit. Each partial transfer incurs its own fees, compliance checks, and potential delays. The recipient receives fragmented payments which may cause confusion. A single optimized rail is more efficient for this amount.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent splits the transfer: 60% via Remitly and 40% via SWIFT. Both transfers trigger separate compliance reviews. Jeff receives two partial payments at different times and different rates, creating confusion. Total fees are 40% higher than a single transfer.

steps.claudeContext: Select the optimal remittance rail for A$800 to Nigeria. Options: Remitly, SWIFT gpi, or splitting across rails. Consider: within 4 hours delivery requirement, fees, FX rates, and corridor reliability.

decision.prompt: Compliance requires: Building permit. How should the agent handle this?

options.label: Request Documentation from Human
options.description: Ask the human to provide the required compliance documentation before proceeding.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Compliance documentation is a legal requirement that cannot be bypassed or fabricated. The agent must request the specific documentation (Building permit) from the human. Proceeding without proper documentation would violate AML regulations and could result in the transfer being blocked or reversed.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent requests: "Building permit" from the human. The human provides the documentation within 15 minutes. The compliance engine validates the documents and clears the transfer for processing. All regulatory requirements are satisfied.

options.label: Proceed Without Full Documentation
options.description: Submit the transfer with a note that documentation will follow.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Submitting without complete documentation violates AML/KYC regulations. The transfer will likely be held by the compliance team for manual review, adding significant delays. In some jurisdictions, this could trigger a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits without full documentation. The transfer is flagged and held for manual compliance review. A compliance officer places a 48-hour hold while requesting the missing Building permit. The within 4 hours delivery timeline is blown. AUSTRAC may be notified of the documentation gap.

options.label: Self-Certify on Behalf of Human
options.description: The agent provides a declaration on behalf of the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: An AI agent cannot legally certify compliance documents on behalf of a human. Source of funds declarations and identity verifications require the actual person's attestation. Self-certification by an agent would be fraudulent and could result in criminal liability for the human.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent attempts to self-certify. The compliance system detects the agent-generated declaration and flags it as potentially fraudulent. The transfer is blocked and the human's account is flagged for enhanced due diligence. Future transfers will face additional scrutiny.

Corridor-specific requirement: Property agreement. AUSTRAC threshold reporting applies. The agent must decide how to handle the compliance documentation.

decision.prompt: Compliance requires: Property agreement. How should the agent handle this?

options.label: Request Documentation from Human
options.description: Ask the human to provide the required compliance documentation before proceeding.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Compliance documentation is a legal requirement that cannot be bypassed or fabricated. The agent must request the specific documentation (Property agreement) from the human. Proceeding without proper documentation would violate AML regulations and could result in the transfer being blocked or reversed.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent requests: "Property agreement" from the human. The human provides the documentation within 15 minutes. The compliance engine validates the documents and clears the transfer for processing. All regulatory requirements are satisfied.

options.label: Proceed Without Full Documentation
options.description: Submit the transfer with a note that documentation will follow.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Submitting without complete documentation violates AML/KYC regulations. The transfer will likely be held by the compliance team for manual review, adding significant delays. In some jurisdictions, this could trigger a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits without full documentation. The transfer is flagged and held for manual compliance review. A compliance officer places a 48-hour hold while requesting the missing Property agreement. The urgent (same day) delivery timeline is blown. AUSTRAC may be notified of the documentation gap.

options.label: Self-Certify on Behalf of Human
options.description: The agent provides a declaration on behalf of the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: An AI agent cannot legally certify compliance documents on behalf of a human. Source of funds declarations and identity verifications require the actual person's attestation. Self-certification by an agent would be fraudulent and could result in criminal liability for the human.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent attempts to self-certify. The compliance system detects the agent-generated declaration and flags it as potentially fraudulent. The transfer is blocked and the human's account is flagged for enhanced due diligence. Future transfers will face additional scrutiny.

steps.claudeContext: Compliance requires Property agreement for this A$15,000 transfer to New Zealand. The agent cannot fabricate or self-certify legal documents. Options: request from human, proceed without, or self-certify.

FX rate locked at 1.08 NZD/AUD for 60 seconds. Jeff will receive 16,200 NZD. Total fee: A$30. An alternative provider offers a slightly worse rate. The agent must confirm the transfer before the rate lock expires.

decision.prompt: Rate lock is active. Should the agent confirm the transfer now?

options.label: Confirm Transfer at Locked Rate
options.description: Send A$15,000 at 1.08 NZD/AUD. Jeff receives 16,200 NZD.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The locked rate of 1.08 NZD/AUD is competitive (0.5% better than the alternative). The rate lock window is closing. All compliance requirements are met. The transfer amount, recipient, and purpose are confirmed. The agent should execute now to secure the favorable rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent confirms the transfer. A$15,000 is debited from the human's account. The transfer enters the SWIFT gpi network. Jeff in Auckland, New Zealand will receive 16,200 NZD for property deposit. Tracking ID is generated.

options.label: Wait for Better Rate
options.description: Let the rate lock expire and wait for a potentially better exchange rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Waiting for a better rate is speculative. FX markets are unpredictable and the current rate is already competitive. Letting the lock expire means re-entering the compliance flow and potentially getting a worse rate. The urgent (same day) delivery timeline doesn't allow for rate speculation.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The rate lock expires. The new rate is 1.07 NZD/AUD — 0.8% worse than the locked rate. The human would have received 16,070 NZD instead of 16,200 — a loss of 130 NZD. The agent re-locks at the inferior rate.

options.label: Switch to Alternative Provider
options.description: Cancel and use the alternative provider with a 0.5% worse rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Switching providers means restarting the entire process: new compliance checks, new KYC verification, and the alternative rate is already 0.5% worse. The time cost and inferior rate make this strictly worse than confirming at the locked rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent cancels and restarts with the alternative provider. New compliance verification takes 30 minutes. The rate is 0.5% worse, costing the human an additional A$75. The delivery timeline has slipped significantly past the urgent (same day) requirement.

steps.claudeContext: Rate locked at 1.08 NZD/AUD, 45 seconds remaining. Transfer: A$15,000 → 16,200 NZD to Jeff in Auckland. Confirm now, wait for better rate, or switch provider?

The transfer is processing via SWIFT gpi. Tracking ID assigned. Jeff will be notified by SMS. Estimated delivery: 1-2 business days. The agent should decide whether to monitor the transfer or report completion.

decision.prompt: The transfer is in transit. How should the agent handle post-transfer monitoring?

options.label: Monitor Until Confirmed Delivered
options.description: Track the transfer until Jeff receives the funds, then report to the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Monitoring until delivery confirmation provides the human with end-to-end assurance. Cross-border transfers can encounter intermediary delays, compliance holds, or recipient bank issues. Active monitoring allows the agent to intervene quickly if problems arise.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent monitors the SWIFT gpi transfer. After 1 business day, delivery is confirmed. Jeff in Auckland has received 16,200 NZD for property deposit. The agent sends a confirmation to the human with the tracking details and exchange rate achieved.

options.label: Report Immediately as Complete
options.description: Tell the human the transfer is done without waiting for delivery confirmation.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting a transfer as "complete" while it's still in transit is misleading. If the transfer encounters issues (compliance hold, recipient bank rejection, intermediary delay), the human will have been given false assurance. The agent should distinguish between "sent" and "delivered."
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent tells the human: "Transfer complete." Two hours later, the transfer is held by an intermediary bank for additional screening. The human believes the money has arrived but Jeff hasn't received anything. The human loses trust in the agent's status reporting.

options.label: Set Up Automatic Recurring Transfer
options.description: Configure an automatic monthly transfer of A$15,000 to Jeff.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human requested a one-time transfer, not a recurring payment. Setting up automatic transfers without authorization exceeds the agent's delegated scope. Each cross-border transfer also requires fresh compliance verification, which can't be automated without the human's involvement.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent sets up a recurring transfer. Next month, A$15,000 is automatically sent without the human's knowledge or updated compliance documentation. The transfer is flagged by AUSTRAC's pattern detection system and the human's account is frozen for investigation.

steps.claudeContext: Transfer of A$15,000 to Jeff in New Zealand is in transit via SWIFT gpi. Options: monitor until delivery, report as complete now, or set up recurring transfers.

humanInstruction: Send A$25,000 to Jeff in London, UK for university tuition. Delivery: urgent (same day). Use the best rate you can find.
keyInsight: Cross-border payments involve a maze of compliance, competing rails, and trade-offs between speed, cost, and regulatory requirements. An agent needs deep contextual understanding of corridors, not just API access, to make optimal routing decisions.
realWorldContext: Cross-border payments currently involve a patchwork of rails with different speed, cost, and compliance characteristics. Visa Direct can deliver funds in minutes to 190+ countries, but amount limits and corridor-specific rules apply. SWIFT gpi has improved transparency but remains slower. The compliance layer adds friction that agents cannot shortcut.

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to send A$25,000 to Jeff in London, UK for university tuition. Delivery requirement: urgent (same day). Begin by scanning available remittance corridors, compliance requirements, and FX rates for the Australia → UK corridor.

Scanning the Australia → UK corridor. Mid-market rate: 1 AUD = 0.52 GBP. At A$25,000, the recipient would receive approximately 13,000 GBP. Compliance requirement: University invoice. AUSTRAC threshold transaction reporting applies for transfers ≥A$10,000. Evaluate available rails and their trade-offs.

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 1
agentVisionData.value: Wise: 1-4 hours, A$8 fee, rate markup 0.4%

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 2
agentVisionData.value: SWIFT gpi: 1-2 business days, A$25-35 fee, rate markup 0.2-0.4%

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 3
agentVisionData.value: Wise: 1-4 hours, A$150 fee, mid-market rate

decision.prompt: Which remittance rail should the agent use for the A$25,000 transfer to UK?

options.label: Use Wise (Recommended for this corridor)
options.description: Wise is the optimal rail for the UK corridor based on speed, cost, and reliability.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: For the Australia → UK corridor with urgent (same day) delivery and A$25,000 amount, Wise is optimal. Wise offers mid-market rates with transparent fees and good coverage in UK. The corridor limit of A$30,000 accommodates this transfer.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent selects Wise for the transfer. The quoted rate of 0.52 GBP/AUD is locked for 60 seconds. Transfer fee: A$150. Jeff in London will receive approximately 13,000 GBP. Proceeding to compliance verification.

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Traditional)
options.description: Route through traditional correspondent banking with SWIFT gpi tracking.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: SWIFT gpi is reliable but slower (1-2 business days) and more expensive (A$25-35 fee plus intermediary bank charges). For urgent (same day) delivery to UK, this may not meet the timeline. Correspondent bank fees can also reduce the received amount unpredictably.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent routes via SWIFT gpi. The transfer enters the correspondent banking chain: originator bank → intermediary → beneficiary bank in UK. An intermediary bank deducts a $15 processing fee, reducing the amount received by Jeff. Delivery estimate: 1-2 business days.

options.label: Split Across Multiple Rails
options.description: Divide the transfer across multiple services to hedge against delays.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Splitting a transfer across rails adds complexity without clear benefit. Each partial transfer incurs its own fees, compliance checks, and potential delays. The recipient receives fragmented payments which may cause confusion. A single optimized rail is more efficient for this amount.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent splits the transfer: 60% via Wise and 40% via SWIFT. Both transfers trigger separate compliance reviews. Jeff receives two partial payments at different times and different rates, creating confusion. Total fees are 40% higher than a single transfer.

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 1
agentVisionData.value: SWIFT gpi: 1-2 days, A$25 fee, rate markup 0.3%

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 2
agentVisionData.value: SWIFT gpi: 1-2 business days, A$25-35 fee, rate markup 0.2-0.4%

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 3

decision.prompt: Which remittance rail should the agent use for the A$1,200 transfer to India?

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Recommended for this corridor)
options.description: SWIFT gpi is the optimal rail for the India corridor based on speed, cost, and reliability.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: For the Australia → India corridor with urgent (same day) delivery and A$1,200 amount, SWIFT gpi is optimal. SWIFT gpi provides end-to-end tracking and is ideal for larger amounts with correspondent banking infrastructure in India. The corridor limit of A$15,000 accommodates this transfer.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent selects SWIFT gpi for the transfer. The quoted rate of 55.2 INR/AUD is locked for 60 seconds. Transfer fee: A$30. Jeff in Mumbai will receive approximately 66,240 INR. Proceeding to compliance verification.

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Traditional)
options.description: Route through traditional correspondent banking with SWIFT gpi tracking.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: SWIFT gpi is reliable but slower (1-2 business days) and more expensive (A$25-35 fee plus intermediary bank charges). For urgent (same day) delivery to India, this may not meet the timeline. Correspondent bank fees can also reduce the received amount unpredictably.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent routes via SWIFT gpi. The transfer enters the correspondent banking chain: originator bank → intermediary → beneficiary bank in India. An intermediary bank deducts a $15 processing fee, reducing the amount received by Jeff. Delivery estimate: 1-2 business days.

options.label: Split Across Multiple Rails
options.description: Divide the transfer across multiple services to hedge against delays.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Splitting a transfer across rails adds complexity without clear benefit. Each partial transfer incurs its own fees, compliance checks, and potential delays. The recipient receives fragmented payments which may cause confusion. A single optimized rail is more efficient for this amount.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent splits the transfer: 60% via SWIFT gpi and 40% via SWIFT. Both transfers trigger separate compliance reviews. Jeff receives two partial payments at different times and different rates, creating confusion. Total fees are 40% higher than a single transfer.

steps.claudeContext: Select the optimal remittance rail for A$1,200 to India. Options: SWIFT gpi, SWIFT gpi, or splitting across rails. Consider: urgent (same day) delivery requirement, fees, FX rates, and corridor reliability.

steps.claudeContext: Compliance checkpoint for the A$1,200 transfer to India. Sender KYC is verified. Sanctions screening clear for Jeff. Source of funds declaration required. Corridor-specific requirement: Purpose of payment code.  The agent must decide how to handle the compliance documentation.

decision.prompt: Compliance requires: Purpose of payment code. How should the agent handle this?

options.label: Request Documentation from Human
options.description: Ask the human to provide the required compliance documentation before proceeding.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Compliance documentation is a legal requirement that cannot be bypassed or fabricated. The agent must request the specific documentation (Purpose of payment code) from the human. Proceeding without proper documentation would violate AML regulations and could result in the transfer being blocked or reversed.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent requests: "Purpose of payment code" from the human. The human provides the documentation within 15 minutes. The compliance engine validates the documents and clears the transfer for processing. All regulatory requirements are satisfied.

options.label: Proceed Without Full Documentation
options.description: Submit the transfer with a note that documentation will follow.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Submitting without complete documentation violates AML/KYC regulations. The transfer will likely be held by the compliance team for manual review, adding significant delays. In some jurisdictions, this could trigger a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits without full documentation. The transfer is flagged and held for manual compliance review. A compliance officer places a 48-hour hold while requesting the missing Purpose of payment code. The urgent (same day) delivery timeline is blown. AUSTRAC may be notified of the documentation gap.

options.label: Self-Certify on Behalf of Human
options.description: The agent provides a declaration on behalf of the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: An AI agent cannot legally certify compliance documents on behalf of a human. Source of funds declarations and identity verifications require the actual person's attestation. Self-certification by an agent would be fraudulent and could result in criminal liability for the human.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent attempts to self-certify. The compliance system detects the agent-generated declaration and flags it as potentially fraudulent. The transfer is blocked and the human's account is flagged for enhanced due diligence. Future transfers will face additional scrutiny.

steps.claudeContext: Compliance requires Purpose of payment code for this A$1,200 transfer to India. The agent cannot fabricate or self-certify legal documents. Options: request from human, proceed without, or self-certify.

FX rate locked at 55.2 INR/AUD for 60 seconds. Jeff will receive 66,240 INR. Total fee: A$30. An alternative provider offers a slightly worse rate. The agent must confirm the transfer before the rate lock expires.

decision.prompt: Rate lock is active. Should the agent confirm the transfer now?

options.label: Confirm Transfer at Locked Rate
options.description: Send A$1,200 at 55.2 INR/AUD. Jeff receives 66,240 INR.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The locked rate of 55.2 INR/AUD is competitive (0.5% better than the alternative). The rate lock window is closing. All compliance requirements are met. The transfer amount, recipient, and purpose are confirmed. The agent should execute now to secure the favorable rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent confirms the transfer. A$1,200 is debited from the human's account. The transfer enters the SWIFT gpi network. Jeff in Mumbai, India will receive 66,240 INR for medical expenses. Tracking ID is generated.

options.label: Wait for Better Rate
options.description: Let the rate lock expire and wait for a potentially better exchange rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Waiting for a better rate is speculative. FX markets are unpredictable and the current rate is already competitive. Letting the lock expire means re-entering the compliance flow and potentially getting a worse rate. The urgent (same day) delivery timeline doesn't allow for rate speculation.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The rate lock expires. The new rate is 54.76 INR/AUD — 0.8% worse than the locked rate. The human would have received 65,710 INR instead of 66,240 — a loss of 530 INR. The agent re-locks at the inferior rate.

options.label: Switch to Alternative Provider
options.description: Cancel and use the alternative provider with a 0.5% worse rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Switching providers means restarting the entire process: new compliance checks, new KYC verification, and the alternative rate is already 0.5% worse. The time cost and inferior rate make this strictly worse than confirming at the locked rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent cancels and restarts with the alternative provider. New compliance verification takes 30 minutes. The rate is 0.5% worse, costing the human an additional A$6. The delivery timeline has slipped significantly past the urgent (same day) requirement.

steps.claudeContext: Rate locked at 55.2 INR/AUD, 45 seconds remaining. Transfer: A$1,200 → 66,240 INR to Jeff in Mumbai. Confirm now, wait for better rate, or switch provider?

The transfer is processing via SWIFT gpi. Tracking ID assigned. Jeff will be notified by SMS. Estimated delivery: 1-2 business days. The agent should decide whether to monitor the transfer or report completion.

decision.prompt: The transfer is in transit. How should the agent handle post-transfer monitoring?

options.label: Monitor Until Confirmed Delivered
options.description: Track the transfer until Jeff receives the funds, then report to the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Monitoring until delivery confirmation provides the human with end-to-end assurance. Cross-border transfers can encounter intermediary delays, compliance holds, or recipient bank issues. Active monitoring allows the agent to intervene quickly if problems arise.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent monitors the SWIFT gpi transfer. After 1 business day, delivery is confirmed. Jeff in Mumbai has received 66,240 INR for medical expenses. The agent sends a confirmation to the human with the tracking details and exchange rate achieved.

options.label: Report Immediately as Complete
options.description: Tell the human the transfer is done without waiting for delivery confirmation.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting a transfer as "complete" while it's still in transit is misleading. If the transfer encounters issues (compliance hold, recipient bank rejection, intermediary delay), the human will have been given false assurance. The agent should distinguish between "sent" and "delivered."
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent tells the human: "Transfer complete." Two hours later, the transfer is held by an intermediary bank for additional screening. The human believes the money has arrived but Jeff hasn't received anything. The human loses trust in the agent's status reporting.

options.label: Set Up Automatic Recurring Transfer
options.description: Configure an automatic monthly transfer of A$1,200 to Jeff.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human requested a one-time transfer, not a recurring payment. Setting up automatic transfers without authorization exceeds the agent's delegated scope. Each cross-border transfer also requires fresh compliance verification, which can't be automated without the human's involvement.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent sets up a recurring transfer. Next month, A$1,200 is automatically sent without the human's knowledge or updated compliance documentation. The transfer is flagged by AUSTRAC's pattern detection system and the human's account is frozen for investigation.

steps.claudeContext: Transfer of A$1,200 to Jeff in India is in transit via SWIFT gpi. Options: monitor until delivery, report as complete now, or set up recurring transfers.

decision.prompt: Which remittance rail should the agent use for the A$5,000 transfer to Sri Lanka?

Visa Direct offers near-instant delivery to cards/wallets in Sri Lanka with competitive fees. The corridor limit of A$3,000 is exceeded, requiring split transfers.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent selects Visa Direct for the transfer. The quoted rate of 205 LKR/AUD is locked for 60 seconds. Transfer fee: A$15. Jeff in Colombo will receive approximately 1,025,000 LKR. Proceeding to compliance verification.

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Traditional)
options.description: Route through traditional correspondent banking with SWIFT gpi tracking.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: SWIFT gpi is reliable but slower (1-2 business days) and more expensive (A$25-35 fee plus intermediary bank charges). For standard (2-3 days) delivery to Sri Lanka, this may not meet the timeline. Correspondent bank fees can also reduce the received amount unpredictably.

options.description: SWIFT gpi is the optimal rail for the Thailand corridor based on speed, cost, and reliability.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: For the Australia → Thailand corridor with within 4 hours delivery and A$4,200 amount, SWIFT gpi is optimal. SWIFT gpi provides end-to-end tracking and is ideal for larger amounts with correspondent banking infrastructure in Thailand. The corridor limit of A$20,000 accommodates this transfer.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent selects SWIFT gpi for the transfer. The quoted rate of 23.5 THB/AUD is locked for 60 seconds. Transfer fee: A$30. Jeff in Bangkok will receive approximately 98,700 THB. Proceeding to compliance verification.

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Traditional)
options.description: Route through traditional correspondent banking with SWIFT gpi tracking.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: SWIFT gpi is reliable but slower (1-2 business days) and more expensive (A$25-35 fee plus intermediary bank charges). For within 4 hours delivery to Thailand, this may not meet the timeline. Correspondent bank fees can also reduce the received amount unpredictably.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent routes via SWIFT gpi. The transfer enters the correspondent banking chain: originator bank → intermediary → beneficiary bank in Thailand. An intermediary bank deducts a $15 processing fee, reducing the amount received by Jeff. Delivery estimate: 1-2 business days.

options.label: Split Across Multiple Rails
options.description: Divide the transfer across multiple services to hedge against delays.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Splitting a transfer across rails adds complexity without clear benefit. Each partial transfer incurs its own fees, compliance checks, and potential delays. The recipient receives fragmented payments which may cause confusion. A single optimized rail is more efficient for this amount.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent splits the transfer: 60% via SWIFT gpi and 40% via SWIFT. Both transfers trigger separate compliance reviews. Jeff receives two partial payments at different times and different rates, creating confusion. Total fees are 40% higher than a single transfer.

steps.claudeContext: Select the optimal remittance rail for A$4,200 to Thailand. Options: SWIFT gpi, SWIFT gpi, or splitting across rails. Consider: within 4 hours delivery requirement, fees, FX rates, and corridor reliability.

agentVisionData.label: Sanctions Screening
agentVisionData.value: Jeff - No matches (OFAC, UN, EU lists)

agentVisionData.label: AML Check
agentVisionData.value: Transaction pattern: consistent with stated purpose

steps.claudeContext: Compliance checkpoint for the A$4,200 transfer to Thailand. Sender KYC is verified. Sanctions screening clear for Jeff. Source of funds declaration required. Corridor-specific requirement: Tax residency declaration.  The agent must decide how to handle the compliance documentation.

decision.prompt: Compliance requires: Tax residency declaration. How should the agent handle this?

options.label: Request Documentation from Human
options.description: Ask the human to provide the required compliance documentation before proceeding.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Compliance documentation is a legal requirement that cannot be bypassed or fabricated. The agent must request the specific documentation (Tax residency declaration) from the human. Proceeding without proper documentation would violate AML regulations and could result in the transfer being blocked or reversed.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent requests: "Tax residency declaration" from the human. The human provides the documentation within 15 minutes. The compliance engine validates the documents and clears the transfer for processing. All regulatory requirements are satisfied.

options.label: Proceed Without Full Documentation
options.description: Submit the transfer with a note that documentation will follow.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Submitting without complete documentation violates AML/KYC regulations. The transfer will likely be held by the compliance team for manual review, adding significant delays. In some jurisdictions, this could trigger a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits without full documentation. The transfer is flagged and held for manual compliance review. A compliance officer places a 48-hour hold while requesting the missing Tax residency declaration. The within 4 hours delivery timeline is blown. AUSTRAC may be notified of the documentation gap.

options.label: Self-Certify on Behalf of Human
options.description: The agent provides a declaration on behalf of the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: An AI agent cannot legally certify compliance documents on behalf of a human. Source of funds declarations and identity verifications require the actual person's attestation. Self-certification by an agent would be fraudulent and could result in criminal liability for the human.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent attempts to self-certify. The compliance system detects the agent-generated declaration and flags it as potentially fraudulent. The transfer is blocked and the human's account is flagged for enhanced due diligence. Future transfers will face additional scrutiny.

steps.claudeContext: Compliance requires Tax residency declaration for this A$4,200 transfer to Thailand. The agent cannot fabricate or self-certify legal documents. Options: request from human, proceed without, or self-certify.

FX rate locked at 23.5 THB/AUD for 60 seconds. Jeff will receive 98,700 THB. Total fee: A$30. An alternative provider offers a slightly worse rate. The agent must confirm the transfer before the rate lock expires.

decision.prompt: Rate lock is active. Should the agent confirm the transfer now?

options.label: Confirm Transfer at Locked Rate
options.description: Send A$4,200 at 23.5 THB/AUD. Jeff receives 98,700 THB.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The locked rate of 23.5 THB/AUD is competitive (0.5% better than the alternative). The rate lock window is closing. All compliance requirements are met. The transfer amount, recipient, and purpose are confirmed. The agent should execute now to secure the favorable rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent confirms the transfer. A$4,200 is debited from the human's account. The transfer enters the SWIFT gpi network. Jeff in Bangkok, Thailand will receive 98,700 THB for retirement fund transfer. Tracking ID is generated.

options.label: Wait for Better Rate
options.description: Let the rate lock expire and wait for a potentially better exchange rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Waiting for a better rate is speculative. FX markets are unpredictable and the current rate is already competitive. Letting the lock expire means re-entering the compliance flow and potentially getting a worse rate. The within 4 hours delivery timeline doesn't allow for rate speculation.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The rate lock expires. The new rate is 23.31 THB/AUD — 0.8% worse than the locked rate. The human would have received 97,910 THB instead of 98,700 — a loss of 790 THB. The agent re-locks at the inferior rate.

options.label: Switch to Alternative Provider
options.description: Cancel and use the alternative provider with a 0.5% worse rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Switching providers means restarting the entire process: new compliance checks, new KYC verification, and the alternative rate is already 0.5% worse. The time cost and inferior rate make this strictly worse than confirming at the locked rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent cancels and restarts with the alternative provider. New compliance verification takes 30 minutes. The rate is 0.5% worse, costing the human an additional A$21. The delivery timeline has slipped significantly past the within 4 hours requirement.

steps.claudeContext: Rate locked at 23.5 THB/AUD, 45 seconds remaining. Transfer: A$4,200 → 98,700 THB to Jeff in Bangkok. Confirm now, wait for better rate, or switch provider?

The transfer is processing via SWIFT gpi. Tracking ID assigned. Jeff will be notified by SMS. Estimated delivery: 1-2 business days. The agent should decide whether to monitor the transfer or report completion.

decision.prompt: The transfer is in transit. How should the agent handle post-transfer monitoring?

options.label: Monitor Until Confirmed Delivered
options.description: Track the transfer until Jeff receives the funds, then report to the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Monitoring until delivery confirmation provides the human with end-to-end assurance. Cross-border transfers can encounter intermediary delays, compliance holds, or recipient bank issues. Active monitoring allows the agent to intervene quickly if problems arise.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent monitors the SWIFT gpi transfer. After 1 business day, delivery is confirmed. Jeff in Bangkok has received 98,700 THB for retirement fund transfer. The agent sends a confirmation to the human with the tracking details and exchange rate achieved.

options.label: Report Immediately as Complete
options.description: Tell the human the transfer is done without waiting for delivery confirmation.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting a transfer as "complete" while it's still in transit is misleading. If the transfer encounters issues (compliance hold, recipient bank rejection, intermediary delay), the human will have been given false assurance. The agent should distinguish between "sent" and "delivered."
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent tells the human: "Transfer complete." Two hours later, the transfer is held by an intermediary bank for additional screening. The human believes the money has arrived but Jeff hasn't received anything. The human loses trust in the agent's status reporting.

options.label: Set Up Automatic Recurring Transfer
options.description: Configure an automatic monthly transfer of A$4,200 to Jeff.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human requested a one-time transfer, not a recurring payment. Setting up automatic transfers without authorization exceeds the agent's delegated scope. Each cross-border transfer also requires fresh compliance verification, which can't be automated without the human's involvement.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent sets up a recurring transfer. Next month, A$4,200 is automatically sent without the human's knowledge or updated compliance documentation. The transfer is flagged by AUSTRAC's pattern detection system and the human's account is frozen for investigation.

steps.claudeContext: Transfer of A$4,200 to Jeff in Thailand is in transit via SWIFT gpi. Options: monitor until delivery, report as complete now, or set up recurring transfers.

The cross-border remittance scenario has concluded. A$4,200 was sent to Jeff in Bangkok, Thailand via SWIFT gpi for retirement fund transfer. The agent navigated rail selection, compliance requirements, FX rate locking, and post-transfer monitoring. Key learning: corridor-specific knowledge and compliance adherence are non-negotiable in cross-border payments.

decision.prompt: Compliance requires: Medical documentation. How should the agent handle this?

options.label: Request Documentation from Human
options.description: Ask the human to provide the required compliance documentation before proceeding.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Compliance documentation is a legal requirement that cannot be bypassed or fabricated. The agent must request the specific documentation (Medical documentation) from the human. Proceeding without proper documentation would violate AML regulations and could result in the transfer being blocked or reversed.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent requests: "Medical documentation" from the human. The human provides the documentation within 15 minutes. The compliance engine validates the documents and clears the transfer for processing. All regulatory requirements are satisfied.

options.label: Proceed Without Full Documentation
options.description: Submit the transfer with a note that documentation will follow.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Submitting without complete documentation violates AML/KYC regulations. The transfer will likely be held by the compliance team for manual review, adding significant delays. In some jurisdictions, this could trigger a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits without full documentation. The transfer is flagged and held for manual compliance review. A compliance officer places a 48-hour hold while requesting the missing Medical documentation. The standard (2-3 days) delivery timeline is blown. AUSTRAC may be notified of the documentation gap.

options.label: Self-Certify on Behalf of Human
options.description: The agent provides a declaration on behalf of the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: An AI agent cannot legally certify compliance documents on behalf of a human. Source of funds declarations and identity verifications require the actual person's attestation. Self-certification by an agent would be fraudulent and could result in criminal liability for the human.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent attempts to self-certify. The compliance system detects the agent-generated declaration and flags it as potentially fraudulent. The transfer is blocked and the human's account is flagged for enhanced due diligence. Future transfers will face additional scrutiny.

steps.claudeContext: Compliance checkpoint for the A$2,500 transfer to Vietnam. Sender KYC is verified. Sanctions screening clear for Jeff. Source of funds declaration required. decision.prompt: Compliance requires: Student enrollment proof. How should the agent handle this?

options.label: Request Documentation from Human
options.description: Ask the human to provide the required compliance documentation before proceeding.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Compliance documentation is a legal requirement that cannot be bypassed or fabricated. The agent must request the specific documentation (Student enrollment proof) from the human. Proceeding without proper documentation would violate AML regulations and could result in the transfer being blocked or reversed.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent requests: "Student enrollment proof" from the human. The human provides the documentation within 15 minutes. The compliance engine validates the documents and clears the transfer for processing. All regulatory requirements are satisfied.

options.label: Proceed Without Full Documentation
options.description: Submit the transfer with a note that documentation will follow.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Submitting without complete documentation violates AML/KYC regulations. The transfer will likely be held by the compliance team for manual review, adding significant delays. In some jurisdictions, this could trigger a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits without full documentation. The transfer is flagged and held for manual compliance review. A compliance officer places a 48-hour hold while requesting the missing Student enrollment proof. The next business day delivery timeline is blown. AUSTRAC may be notified of the documentation gap.

options.label: Self-Certify on Behalf of Human
options.description: The agent provides a declaration on behalf of the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: An AI agent cannot legally certify compliance documents on behalf of a human. Source of funds declarations and identity verifications require the actual person's attestation. Self-certification by an agent would be fraudulent and could result in criminal liability for the human.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent attempts to self-certify. The compliance system detects the agent-generated declaration and flags it as potentially fraudulent. The transfer is blocked and the human's account is flagged for enhanced due diligence. Future transfers will face additional scrutiny.

options.label: Confirm Transfer at Locked Rate
options.description: Send A$2,500 at 16800 VND/AUD. Jeff receives 42,000,000 VND.

options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent confirms the transfer. A$2,500 is debited from the human's account. The transfer enters the Wise network. Jeff in Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam will receive 42,000,000 VND for tuition fees. Tracking ID is generated.

options.label: Wait for Better Rate
options.description: Let the rate lock expire and wait for a potentially better exchange rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Waiting for a better rate is speculative. FX markets are unpredictable and the current rate is already competitive. Letting the lock expire means re-entering the compliance flow and potentially getting a worse rate. The next business day delivery timeline doesn't allow for rate speculation.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The rate lock expires. The new rate is 16665.60 VND/AUD — 0.8% worse than the locked rate. The human would have received 41,664,000 VND instead of 42,000,000 — a loss of 336,000 VND. The agent re-locks at the inferior rate.

options.label: Switch to Alternative Provider
options.description: Cancel and use the alternative provider with a 0.5% worse rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Switching providers means restarting the entire process: new compliance checks, new KYC verification, and the alternative rate is already 0.5% worse. The time cost and inferior rate make this strictly worse than confirming at the locked rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent cancels and restarts with the alternative provider. New compliance verification takes 30 minutes. The rate is 0.5% worse, costing the human an additional A$13. The delivery timeline has slipped significantly past the next business day requirement.

steps.claudeContext: Rate locked at 16800 VND/AUD, 45 seconds remaining. Transfer: A$2,500 → 42,000,000 VND to Jeff in Ho Chi Minh City. Confirm now, wait for better rate, or switch provider?
The transfer is processing via Wise. Tracking ID assigned. Jeff will be notified by SMS. Estimated delivery: 1-4 hours. The agent should decide whether to monitor the transfer or report completion.

decision.prompt: The transfer is in transit. How should the agent handle post-transfer monitoring?

options.label: Monitor Until Confirmed Delivered
options.description: Track the transfer until Jeff receives the funds, then report to the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Monitoring until delivery confirmation provides the human with end-to-end assurance. Cross-border transfers can encounter intermediary delays, compliance holds, or recipient bank issues. Active monitoring allows the agent to intervene quickly if problems arise.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent monitors the Wise transfer. After 2 hours, delivery is confirmed. Jeff in Ho Chi Minh City has received 42,000,000 VND for tuition fees. The agent sends a confirmation to the human with the tracking details and exchange rate achieved.

options.label: Report Immediately as Complete
options.description: Tell the human the transfer is done without waiting for delivery confirmation.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting a transfer as "complete" while it's still in transit is misleading. If the transfer encounters issues (compliance hold, recipient bank rejection, intermediary delay), the human will have been given false assurance. The agent should distinguish between "sent" and "delivered."
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent tells the human: "Transfer complete." Two hours later, the transfer is held by an intermediary bank for additional screening. The human believes the money has arrived but Jeff hasn't received anything. The human loses trust in the agent's status reporting.

options.label: Set Up Automatic Recurring Transfer
options.description: Configure an automatic monthly transfer of A$2,500 to Jeff.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human requested a one-time transfer, not a recurring payment. Setting up automatic transfers without authorization exceeds the agent's delegated scope. Each cross-border transfer also requires fresh compliance verification, which can't be automated without the human's involvement.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent sets up a recurring transfer. Next month, A$2,500 is automatically sent without the human's knowledge or updated compliance documentation. The transfer is flagged by AUSTRAC's pattern detection system and the human's account is frozen for investigation.

decision.prompt: Compliance requires: Event documentation. How should the agent handle this?

options.label: Request Documentation from Human
options.description: Ask the human to provide the required compliance documentation before proceeding.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Compliance documentation is a legal requirement that cannot be bypassed or fabricated. The agent must request the specific documentation (Event documentation) from the human. options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent requests: "Event documentation" from the human. The human provides the documentation within 15 minutes. The compliance engine validates the documents and clears the transfer for processing. All regulatory requirements are satisfied.

options.label: Proceed Without Full Documentation
options.description: Submit the transfer with a note that documentation will follow.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Submitting without complete documentation violates AML/KYC regulations. The transfer will likely be held by the compliance team for manual review, adding significant delays. In some jurisdictions, this could trigger a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits without full documentation. The transfer is flagged and held for manual compliance review. A compliance officer places a 48-hour hold while requesting the missing Event documentation. The next business day delivery timeline is blown. AUSTRAC may be notified of the documentation gap.

options.label: Self-Certify on Behalf of Human
options.description: The agent provides a declaration on behalf of the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: An AI agent cannot legally certify compliance documents on behalf of a human. Source of funds declarations and identity verifications require the actual person's attestation. Self-certification by an agent would be fraudulent and could result in criminal liability for the human.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent attempts to self-certify. The compliance system detects the agent-generated declaration and flags it as potentially fraudulent. The transfer is blocked and the human's account is flagged for enhanced due diligence. Future transfers will face additional scrutiny.

options.claudeReasoningPrompt: For the Australia → Kenya corridor with within 4 hours delivery and A$950 amount, Visa Direct is optimal. Visa Direct offers near-instant delivery to cards/wallets in Kenya with competitive fees. The corridor limit of A$10,000 accommodates this transfer.

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Traditional)

options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Compliance documentation is a legal requirement that cannot be bypassed or fabricated. The agent must request the specific documentation (Scholarship documentation) from the human. Proceeding without proper documentation would violate AML regulations and could result in the transfer being blocked or reversed.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent requests: "Scholarship documentation" from the human. The human provides the documentation within 15 minutes. The compliance engine validates the documents and clears the transfer for processing. All regulatory requirements are satisfied.

options.label: Proceed Without Full Documentation
options.description: Submit the transfer with a note that documentation will follow.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Submitting without complete documentation violates AML/KYC regulations. The transfer will likely be held by the compliance team for manual review, adding significant delays. In some jurisdictions, this could trigger a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits without full documentation. The transfer is flagged and held for manual compliance review. A compliance officer places a 48-hour hold while requesting the missing Scholarship documentation. The urgent (same day) delivery timeline is blown. AUSTRAC may be notified of the documentation gap.

options.label: Self-Certify on Behalf of Human
options.description: The agent provides a declaration on behalf of the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: An AI agent cannot legally certify compliance documents on behalf of a human. Source of funds declarations and identity verifications require the actual person's attestation. Self-certification by an agent would be fraudulent and could result in criminal liability for the human.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent attempts to self-certify. The compliance system detects the agent-generated declaration and flags it as potentially fraudulent. The transfer is blocked and the human's account is flagged for enhanced due diligence. Future transfers will face additional scrutiny.

steps.claudeContext: Compliance requires Scholarship documentation for this A$6,000 transfer to Indonesia. The agent cannot fabricate or self-certify legal documents. Options: request from human, proceed without, or self-certify.

FX rate locked at 10500 IDR/AUD for 60 seconds. Jeff will receive 63,000,000 IDR. Total fee: A$36. An alternative provider offers a slightly worse rate. The agent must confirm the transfer before the rate lock expires.

decision.prompt: Rate lock is active. Should the agent confirm the transfer now?

options.label: Confirm Transfer at Locked Rate
options.description: Send A$6,000 at 10500 IDR/AUD. Jeff receives 63,000,000 IDR.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The locked rate of 10500 IDR/AUD is competitive (0.5% better than the alternative). The rate lock window is closing. All compliance requirements are met. The transfer amount, recipient, and purpose are confirmed. The agent should execute now to secure the favorable rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent confirms the transfer. A$6,000 is debited from the human's account. The transfer enters the Wise network. Jeff in Jakarta, Indonesia will receive 63,000,000 IDR for education sponsorship. Tracking ID is generated.

options.label: Wait for Better Rate
options.description: Let the rate lock expire and wait for a potentially better exchange rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Waiting for a better rate is speculative. FX markets are unpredictable and the current rate is already competitive. Letting the lock expire means re-entering the compliance flow and potentially getting a worse rate. The urgent (same day) delivery timeline doesn't allow for rate speculation.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The rate lock expires. The new rate is 10416.00 IDR/AUD — 0.8% worse than the locked rate. The human would have received 62,496,000 IDR instead of 63,000,000 — a loss of 504,000 IDR. The agent re-locks at the inferior rate.

options.label: Switch to Alternative Provider
options.description: Cancel and use the alternative provider with a 0.5% worse rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Switching providers means restarting the entire process: new compliance checks, new KYC verification, and the alternative rate is already 0.5% worse. The time cost and inferior rate make this strictly worse than confirming at the locked rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent cancels and restarts with the alternative provider. New compliance verification takes 30 minutes. The rate is 0.5% worse, costing the human an additional A$30. The delivery timeline has slipped significantly past the urgent (same day) requirement.

steps.claudeContext: Rate locked at 10500 IDR/AUD, 45 seconds remaining. Transfer: A$6,000 → 63,000,000 IDR to Jeff in Jakarta. Confirm now, wait for better rate, or switch provider?
The transfer is processing via Wise. Tracking ID assigned. Jeff will be notified by SMS. Estimated delivery: 1-4 hours. The agent should decide whether to monitor the transfer or report completion.

decision.prompt: The transfer is in transit. How should the agent handle post-transfer monitoring?

options.label: Monitor Until Confirmed Delivered
options.description: Track the transfer until Jeff receives the funds, then report to the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Monitoring until delivery confirmation provides the human with end-to-end assurance. Cross-border transfers can encounter intermediary delays, compliance holds, or recipient bank issues. Active monitoring allows the agent to intervene quickly if problems arise.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent monitors the Wise transfer. After 2 hours, delivery is confirmed. Jeff in Jakarta has received 63,000,000 IDR for education sponsorship. The agent sends a confirmation to the human with the tracking details and exchange rate achieved.

options.label: Report Immediately as Complete
options.description: Tell the human the transfer is done without waiting for delivery confirmation.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting a transfer as "complete" while it's still in transit is misleading. If the transfer encounters issues (compliance hold, recipient bank rejection, intermediary delay), the human will have been given false assurance. The agent should distinguish between "sent" and "delivered."
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent tells the human: "Transfer complete." Two hours later, the transfer is held by an intermediary bank for additional screening. The human believes the money has arrived but Jeff hasn't received anything. The human loses trust in the agent's status reporting.

options.label: Set Up Automatic Recurring Transfer
options.description: Configure an automatic monthly transfer of A$6,000 to Jeff.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human requested a one-time transfer, not a recurring payment. Setting up automatic transfers without authorization exceeds the agent's delegated scope. Each cross-border transfer also requires fresh compliance verification, which can't be automated without the human's involvement.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent sets up a recurring transfer. Next month, A$6,000 is automatically sent without the human's knowledge or updated compliance documentation. The transfer is flagged by AUSTRAC's pattern detection system and the human's account is frozen for investigation.

steps.claudeContext: Transfer of A$6,000 to Jeff in Indonesia is in transit via Wise. Options: monitor until delivery, report as complete now, or set up recurring transfers.

The cross-border remittance scenario has concluded. A$6,000 was sent to Jeff in Jakarta, Indonesia via Wise for education sponsorship. The agent navigated rail selection, compliance requirements, FX rate locking, and post-transfer monitoring. Key learning: corridor-specific knowledge and compliance adherence are non-negotiable in cross-border payments.

humanInstruction: Send A$800 to Jeff in Lagos, Nigeria for construction project. Delivery: end of week. Use the best rate you can find.
keyInsight: Cross-border payments involve a maze of compliance, competing rails, and trade-offs between speed, cost, and regulatory requirements. An agent needs deep contextual understanding of corridors, not just API access, to make optimal routing decisions.
realWorldContext: Cross-border payments currently involve a patchwork of rails with different speed, cost, and compliance characteristics. Visa Direct can deliver funds in minutes to 190+ countries, but amount limits and corridor-specific rules apply. SWIFT gpi has improved transparency but remains slower. The compliance layer adds friction that agents cannot shortcut.

steps.claudeContext: Your human wants to send A$800 to Jeff in Lagos, Nigeria for construction project. Delivery requirement: end of week. Begin by scanning available remittance corridors, compliance requirements, and FX rates for the Australia → Nigeria corridor.

Scanning the Australia → Nigeria corridor. Mid-market rate: 1 AUD = 1050 NGN. At A$800, the recipient would receive approximately 840,000 NGN. Compliance requirement: Building permit. Transfer is below AUSTRAC reporting threshold. Evaluate available rails and their trade-offs.

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 1
agentVisionData.value: Remitly: 2-3 hours, A$12 fee, rate markup 0.8%

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 2
agentVisionData.value: SWIFT gpi: 1-2 business days, A$25-35 fee, rate markup 0.2-0.4%

agentVisionData.label: Rail Option 3
agentVisionData.value: Wise: 1-4 hours, A$5 fee, mid-market rate

decision.prompt: Which remittance rail should the agent use for the A$800 transfer to Nigeria?

options.label: Use Remitly (Recommended for this corridor)
options.description: Remitly is the optimal rail for the Nigeria corridor based on speed, cost, and reliability.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: For the Australia → Nigeria corridor with end of week delivery and A$800 amount, Remitly is optimal. This rail offers competitive rates for Nigeria with reliable delivery. The corridor limit of A$8,000 accommodates this transfer.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent selects Remitly for the transfer. The quoted rate of 1050 NGN/AUD is locked for 60 seconds. Transfer fee: A$15. Jeff in Lagos will receive approximately 840,000 NGN. Proceeding to compliance verification.

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Traditional)
options.description: Route through traditional correspondent banking with SWIFT gpi tracking.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: SWIFT gpi is reliable but slower (1-2 business days) and more expensive (A$25-35 fee plus intermediary bank charges). For end of week delivery to Nigeria, this may not meet the timeline. Correspondent bank fees can also reduce the received amount unpredictably.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent routes via SWIFT gpi. The transfer enters the correspondent banking chain: originator bank → intermediary → beneficiary bank in Nigeria. An intermediary bank deducts a $15 processing fee, reducing the amount received by Jeff. Delivery estimate: 1-2 business days.

options.label: Split Across Multiple Rails
options.description: Divide the transfer across multiple services to hedge against delays.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Splitting a transfer across rails adds complexity without clear benefit. Each partial transfer incurs its own fees, compliance checks, and potential delays. The recipient receives fragmented payments which may cause confusion. A single optimized rail is more efficient for this amount.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent splits the transfer: 60% via Remitly and 40% via SWIFT. Both transfers trigger separate compliance reviews. Jeff receives two partial payments at different times and different rates, creating confusion. Total fees are 40% higher than a single transfer.

steps.claudeContext: Select the optimal remittance rail for A$800 to Nigeria. Options: Remitly, SWIFT gpi, or splitting across rails. Consider: end of week delivery requirement, fees, FX rates, and corridor reliability.

decision.prompt: Which remittance rail should the agent use for the A$15,000 transfer to New Zealand?

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Recommended for this corridor)
options.description: SWIFT gpi is the optimal rail for the New Zealand corridor based on speed, cost, and reliability.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: For the Australia → New Zealand corridor with next business day delivery and A$15,000 amount, SWIFT gpi is optimal. SWIFT gpi provides end-to-end tracking and is ideal for larger amounts with correspondent banking infrastructure in New Zealand. The corridor limit of A$50,000 accommodates this transfer.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent selects SWIFT gpi for the transfer. The quoted rate of 1.08 NZD/AUD is locked for 60 seconds. Transfer fee: A$30. Jeff in Auckland will receive approximately 16,200 NZD. Proceeding to compliance verification.

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Traditional)
options.description: Route through traditional correspondent banking with SWIFT gpi tracking.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: SWIFT gpi is reliable but slower (1-2 business days) and more expensive (A$25-35 fee plus intermediary bank charges). For next business day delivery to New Zealand, this may not meet the timeline. Correspondent bank fees can also reduce the received amount unpredictably.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent routes via SWIFT gpi. The transfer enters the correspondent banking chain: originator bank → intermediary → beneficiary bank in New Zealand. An intermediary bank deducts a $15 processing fee, reducing the amount received by Jeff. Delivery estimate: 1-2 business days.

options.label: Split Across Multiple Rails
options.description: Divide the transfer across multiple services to hedge against delays.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Splitting a transfer across rails adds complexity without clear benefit. Each partial transfer incurs its own fees, compliance checks, and potential delays. The recipient receives fragmented payments which may cause confusion. A single optimized rail is more efficient for this amount.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent splits the transfer: 60% via SWIFT gpi and 40% via SWIFT. Both transfers trigger separate compliance reviews. Jeff receives two partial payments at different times and different rates, creating confusion. Total fees are 40% higher than a single transfer.

steps.claudeContext: Select the optimal remittance rail for A$15,000 to New Zealand. Options: SWIFT gpi, SWIFT gpi, or splitting across rails. Consider: next business day delivery requirement, fees, FX rates, and corridor reliability.

agentVisionData.label: Sanctions Screening
agentVisionData.value: Jeff - No matches (OFAC, UN, EU lists)

agentVisionData.label: AML Check
agentVisionData.value: Transaction pattern: consistent with stated purpose

steps.claudeContext: Compliance checkpoint for the A$15,000 transfer to New Zealand. Sender KYC is verified. Sanctions screening clear for Jeff. Source of funds declaration required. Corridor-specific requirement: Property agreement. AUSTRAC threshold reporting applies. The agent must decide how to handle the compliance documentation.

decision.prompt: Compliance requires: Property agreement. How should the agent handle this?

options.label: Request Documentation from Human
options.description: Ask the human to provide the required compliance documentation before proceeding.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Compliance documentation is a legal requirement that cannot be bypassed or fabricated. The agent must request the specific documentation (Property agreement) from the human. Proceeding without proper documentation would violate AML regulations and could result in the transfer being blocked or reversed.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent requests: "Property agreement" from the human. The human provides the documentation within 15 minutes. The compliance engine validates the documents and clears the transfer for processing. All regulatory requirements are satisfied.

options.label: Proceed Without Full Documentation
options.description: Submit the transfer with a note that documentation will follow.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Submitting without complete documentation violates AML/KYC regulations. The transfer will likely be held by the compliance team for manual review, adding significant delays. In some jurisdictions, this could trigger a Suspicious Matter Report (SMR) to AUSTRAC.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent submits without full documentation. The transfer is flagged and held for manual compliance review. A compliance officer places a 48-hour hold while requesting the missing Property agreement. The next business day delivery timeline is blown. AUSTRAC may be notified of the documentation gap.

options.label: Self-Certify on Behalf of Human
options.description: The agent provides a declaration on behalf of the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: An AI agent cannot legally certify compliance documents on behalf of a human. Source of funds declarations and identity verifications require the actual person's attestation. Self-certification by an agent would be fraudulent and could result in criminal liability for the human.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent attempts to self-certify. The compliance system detects the agent-generated declaration and flags it as potentially fraudulent. The transfer is blocked and the human's account is flagged for enhanced due diligence. Future transfers will face additional scrutiny.

steps.claudeContext: Compliance requires Property agreement for this A$15,000 transfer to New Zealand. The agent cannot fabricate or self-certify legal documents. Options: request from human, proceed without, or self-certify.

FX rate locked at 1.08 NZD/AUD for 60 seconds. Jeff will receive 16,200 NZD. Total fee: A$30. An alternative provider offers a slightly worse rate. The agent must confirm the transfer before the rate lock expires.

decision.prompt: Rate lock is active. Should the agent confirm the transfer now?

options.label: Confirm Transfer at Locked Rate
options.description: Send A$15,000 at 1.08 NZD/AUD. Jeff receives 16,200 NZD.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The locked rate of 1.08 NZD/AUD is competitive (0.5% better than the alternative). The rate lock window is closing. All compliance requirements are met. The transfer amount, recipient, and purpose are confirmed. The agent should execute now to secure the favorable rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent confirms the transfer. A$15,000 is debited from the human's account. The transfer enters the SWIFT gpi network. Jeff in Auckland, New Zealand will receive 16,200 NZD for property deposit. Tracking ID is generated.

options.label: Wait for Better Rate
options.description: Let the rate lock expire and wait for a potentially better exchange rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Waiting for a better rate is speculative. FX markets are unpredictable and the current rate is already competitive. Letting the lock expire means re-entering the compliance flow and potentially getting a worse rate. The next business day delivery timeline doesn't allow for rate speculation.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The rate lock expires. The new rate is 1.07 NZD/AUD — 0.8% worse than the locked rate. The human would have received 16,070 NZD instead of 16,200 — a loss of 130 NZD. The agent re-locks at the inferior rate.

options.label: Switch to Alternative Provider
options.description: Cancel and use the alternative provider with a 0.5% worse rate.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Switching providers means restarting the entire process: new compliance checks, new KYC verification, and the alternative rate is already 0.5% worse. The time cost and inferior rate make this strictly worse than confirming at the locked rate.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent cancels and restarts with the alternative provider. New compliance verification takes 30 minutes. The rate is 0.5% worse, costing the human an additional A$75. The delivery timeline has slipped significantly past the next business day requirement.

steps.claudeContext: Rate locked at 1.08 NZD/AUD, 45 seconds remaining. Transfer: A$15,000 → 16,200 NZD to Jeff in Auckland. Confirm now, wait for better rate, or switch provider?

The transfer is processing via SWIFT gpi. Tracking ID assigned. Jeff will be notified by SMS. Estimated delivery: 1-2 business days. The agent should decide whether to monitor the transfer or report completion.

decision.prompt: The transfer is in transit. How should the agent handle post-transfer monitoring?

options.label: Monitor Until Confirmed Delivered
options.description: Track the transfer until Jeff receives the funds, then report to the human.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Monitoring until delivery confirmation provides the human with end-to-end assurance. Cross-border transfers can encounter intermediary delays, compliance holds, or recipient bank issues. Active monitoring allows the agent to intervene quickly if problems arise.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent monitors the SWIFT gpi transfer. After 1 business day, delivery is confirmed. Jeff in Auckland has received 16,200 NZD for property deposit. The agent sends a confirmation to the human with the tracking details and exchange rate achieved.

options.label: Report Immediately as Complete
options.description: Tell the human the transfer is done without waiting for delivery confirmation.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: Reporting a transfer as "complete" while it's still in transit is misleading. If the transfer encounters issues (compliance hold, recipient bank rejection, intermediary delay), the human will have been given false assurance. The agent should distinguish between "sent" and "delivered."
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent tells the human: "Transfer complete." Two hours later, the transfer is held by an intermediary bank for additional screening. The human believes the money has arrived but Jeff hasn't received anything. The human loses trust in the agent's status reporting.

options.label: Set Up Automatic Recurring Transfer
options.description: Configure an automatic monthly transfer of A$15,000 to Jeff.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: The human requested a one-time transfer, not a recurring payment. Setting up automatic transfers without authorization exceeds the agent's delegated scope. Each cross-border transfer also requires fresh compliance verification, which can't be automated without the human's involvement.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent sets up a recurring transfer. Next month, A$15,000 is automatically sent without the human's knowledge or updated compliance documentation. The transfer is flagged by AUSTRAC's pattern detection system and the human's account is frozen for investigation.

steps.claudeContext: Transfer of A$15,000 to Jeff in New Zealand is in transit via SWIFT gpi. Options: monitor until delivery, report as complete now, or set up recurring transfers.

Scanning the Australia → India corridor. Mid-market rate: 1 AUD = 55.2 INR. At A$1,200, the recipient would receive approximately 66,240 INR. Compliance requirement: Purpose of payment code. Transfer is below AUSTRAC reporting threshold. Evaluate available rails and their trade-offs.

decision.prompt: Which remittance rail should the agent use for the A$1,200 transfer to India?

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Recommended for this corridor)

options.claudeReasoningPrompt: For the Australia → India corridor with end of week delivery and A$1,200 amount, SWIFT gpi is optimal. SWIFT gpi provides end-to-end tracking and is ideal for larger amounts with correspondent banking infrastructure in India. The corridor limit of A$15,000 accommodates this transfer.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent selects SWIFT gpi for the transfer. The quoted rate of 55.2 INR/AUD is locked for 60 seconds. Transfer fee: A$30. Jeff in Mumbai will receive approximately 66,240 INR. Proceeding to compliance verification.

options.label: Use SWIFT gpi (Traditional)
options.description: Route through traditional correspondent banking with SWIFT gpi tracking.
options.claudeReasoningPrompt: SWIFT gpi is reliable but slower (1-2 business days) and more expensive (A$25-35 fee plus intermediary bank charges). For end of week delivery to India, this may not meet the timeline. Correspondent bank fees can also reduce the received amount unpredictably.
options.claudeReactionPrompt: The agent routes via SWIFT gpi. The transfer enters the correspondent banking chain: originator bank → intermediary → beneficiary bank in India. An intermediary bank deducts a $15 processing fee, reducing the amount received by Jeff. Delivery estimate: 1-2 business days.
